coredata.dc:title: New free-standing and flexible PVA/Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanocomposite films with promising power factor and thermoelectric power applications
coredata.dc:creator: El-Shamy, Ahmed Gamal
coredata.dc:description: Recently, flexible nanocomposite thermoelectric materials, especially inorganic polymers, have attracted increasing attention. In this paper, we successfully fabricated novel P-type thermoelectric PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films through the solution casting technique. Zero-dimension CQDs nanoparticles were first prepared by the microwave heating method and then injected into the PVA matrix to achieve free-standing films. PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Thermoelectric properties such as electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, and thermal conductivity of the prepared films were studied. XRD patterns showed the effect of CQDs nanoparticles on the crystallinity of the nanocomposite. The average size of CQDs nanoparticles was found to be 4.5 nm. The PVA/CQDs nanocomposite has a high conductivity (30.3 S/cm), an excellent power factor (15.2 μW/m K2), and a high figure of merit (0.005) with CQDs content of 2 wt%. Finally, these nanocomposites have a promising potential application in thermoelectric devices because they are economical and easy to scale up.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PVA
CQD nanoparticles
Melting point
Tensile strength
Thermoelectric power

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: X-ray analysis and TEM and FTIR studies
Mechanical tensile strength and melting point
Figure of merit of PVA/CQDs nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) shows the XRD patterns of the samples (CQDs, PCQ0 and PCQ4, respectively), (B) shows the interaction between the CQDs nanoparticles and the PVA matrix, (C) shows the TEM image of the as-synthesized CQDs nanoparticles. (D) shows the FTIR spectrum of the as-synthesized CQDs nanoparticles.
Shows (A) Young modulus (B) Tensile strength (C) Strain at break and (D) Melting point values of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films as a function of the CQDs concentration.

(a), shows the thermoelectric power of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films as a function of absolute temperature. 3(b), shows the Seebeck coefficient of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films as a function of CQDs concentration at room temperature.

(A) shows the thermal conductivity of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films as a function of the absolute temperature. (B) shows the thermal conductivity of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films as a function of the CQDs concentration at room temperature.
Shows the power factor of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films as a function of CQDs concentration.
(A) shows the electrical conductivity of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films against the different temperatures. (B) shows the electrical conductivity at room temperature.
Shows the dependence of the power factor on the electrical conductivity of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films.
Shows the figure of merit of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films as a function of CQDs concentration.
(A) shows the home-made device of the Seebeck coefficient measurements, (B) the home-made device of the thermal conductivity measurements.
Illustrates the FTIR bands with their assignments.
Thermoelectric properties of some organic polymer nanocomposites and inorganic materials.

head.ce:title: New free-standing and flexible PVA/Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanocomposite films with promising power factor and thermoelectric power applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Suez Canal University, Ismailia, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Suez Canal University, Ismailia, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, flexible nanocomposite thermoelectric materials, especially inorganic polymers, have attracted increasing attention. In this paper, we successfully fabricated novel P-type thermoelectric PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films through the solution casting technique. Zero-dimension CQDs nanoparticles were first prepared by the microwave heating method and then injected into the PVA matrix to achieve free-standing films. PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Thermoelectric properties such as electrical conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, and thermal conductivity of the prepared films were studied. XRD patterns showed the effect of CQDs nanoparticles on the crystallinity of the nanocomposite. The average size of CQDs nanoparticles was found to be 4.5 nm. The PVA/CQDs nanocomposite has a high conductivity (30.3 S/cm), an excellent power factor (15.2 μW/m K2), and a high figure of merit (0.005) with CQDs content of 2 wt%. Finally, these nanocomposites have a promising potential application in thermoelectric devices because they are economical and easy to scale up.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, polymer nanocomposite-based thermoelectric materials have attracted increasing attention because of their potential functional applications in our daily life. In fact, these thermoelectric materials are used to convert thermal energy directly into electrical energy. The performance of these materials can be described using the figure of merit ZT = S2σT/k [1,2]. To obtain materials with high figure of merit (ZT), the following conditions should be satisfied: the materials must have (i) high Seebeck coefficient, (ii) high electrical conductivity, and (iii) low thermal conductivity [3]. Research on thermoelectric materials was initially conducted with classical inorganic materials (e.g., PbTe and Bi2Te3) because of their enhanced thermoelectric responses. However, these inorganic materials showed significant shortcomings such as the high cost of raw materials, toxicity, limited natural resources, and potential for heavy metal pollution [4]. Moreover, they usually require a complicated preparation process, which increases cost. It is difficult to integrate these materials into thermoelectric devices and to recycle. In addition, they are usually brittle, which makes them unsuitable for development of flexible and wearable devices [5]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to look for economical, environmental friendly, flexible, and high-performance thermoelectric materials. Further, a new class of materials (carbon materials) replaced the classical inorganic materials because of the unique electronic properties of carbon materials (such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, and others); they have been largely considered for different functional thermoelectric applications and others [6]. Moreover, adjacent carbon materials have a high junction resistance between them, and hence, the bulk conductivity (S/cm) of the films based on carbon materials is limited. Additionally, carbon materials have a high thermal conductivity that produces a low figure of merit, thereby resulting in precluding its application as thermoelectric materials. Therefore, conducting polymers were used to incorporate carbon materials to overcome the above limitation and enhance the figure of merit performance, as conductive nanocomposites own exclusive benefits such as high conductivity, lightweight, easy handling, low thermal conductivity, and so on [7]. Recently, a major progress has been achieved in conducting polymers either alone or doped with fillers as thermoelectric materials. For example, hybrid materials based on polyaniline (PANI)/graphene poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly (styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS)/PANI/DWCNT were prepared by the layer-by-layer assembly technique, and it was found that for the 80 quad layers of PEDOT:PSS, these hybrid materials showed a Seebeck coefficient of 120 μV/K, a high electrical conductivity of 19 × 102 S/cm, and a power factor of 2710 μW/mK2 [5]. In addition, post-treatment of the films using acids and bases demonstrated a high power factor of 334 μW/mK2 for PEDOT:PSS [8]. The use of conducting polymers (PEDOT and PANI) is an effective approach to try to solve the limitations of thermoelectric materials because these polymers have high electrical conductivity, optical transparency, environmental stability, low density, adequate flexibility, low thermal conductivity, excellent thermal stability, and easy handling. However, because these polymers have limited solvent solubility and complicated preparation methods, they are normally emulsified with PSS in water, which restricts their application to some extent [9,10]. In addition, the presence of high charge carriers contribute to the increase in the electronic thermal conductivity (κ electron) of the polymers, and this leads to some elevation in the thermal conductivity. Recently, doping of dielectric polymers with carbon and other inorganic materials is considered as a method to adjust and tune the Seebeck coefficient and electrical conductivity and solve the above limitation. Recently, PVA was doped with graphene (PVA:GO 10:90%) to enhance the thermoelectric performance, and for 1000 layers of graphene, the Seebeck coefficient was enhanced to 90 μV/K, which is 30% higher than that of graphene alone [4]. However, the relationship between the electrical conductivity and the Seebeck coefficient or that between the electrical conductivity and the thermal conductivity in these studies is puzzling because an increase in the Seebeck coefficient is usually accompanied by a decrease in the electrical conductivity, while the increase in the electrical conductivity leads to an increase in the thermal conductivity. In addition to complicated preparation methods, the thermoelectric materials have the following disadvantages: (1) short-term stability; poor mechanical properties (strength, Young's modulus, etc.); (2) weak electrical contacts between the polymer–filler interface at the active thermoelectric regions and between the polymer and filler, which is interconnected for device application; (3) selection of the suitable mode for doping of polymers and suitable polymer/filler composites, which include nano-scaled polymer/filler materials; and (4) energy-filtering effect [11]. Hence, this paves way for an in-depth study on thermoelectric materials to find a suitable material with favorable mechanical and thermoelectric properties.

Thus, to address these aims, we introduce in this paper a new, flexible, economical, easy-to-scale-up, and very simple nanocomposite based on PVA and CQDs nanoparticles with high mechanical and thermal parameters and thermoelectric performance.

In fact, PVA polymer is a special polymer with unique properties for potential applications because of its ease of preparation, stability under adverse environment, low cost, and reversible control of its thermoelectric properties [12–14]. Current studies have recognized that the insertion of different nanomaterials into the matrix of PVA enables to modify the electrical and thermoelectric properties of the nanocomposite. Furthermore, this can enhance the Seebeck coefficient of the nanocomposites at the interface regions by the energy-filtering effect [1,15]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have also appeared as one of the most important zero-dimensional materials in the last decade because of their desirable optical, electronic, and spin properties influenced by the quantum confinement phenomenon and edge effect [16–18]. Recently, a number of methods have been developed to synthesize CQDs, including chemical synthesis, laser ablation [19], electrochemical methods, and hydrothermal treatment [20]. In fact, CQDs have been regarded as a significant candidate for potential functional applications in bioimaging [21], light-emitting devices [18,22], photovoltaics [23], and other applications.

It is expected that the interaction between CQDs nanoparticles and PVA matrix produces hydrogen bonding, electrostatic, and chemical bonding interactions that can improve the thermoelectric properties of this composite.

To the best of our knowledge, no reports on the thermoelectric properties of PVA/CQDs nanocomposites are available in the literature. In this work, we report in this study and for the first time an easy, inexpensive, and rapid synthesis procedure in which the PVA/CQD nanocomposite can be prepared by casting techniques supported by microwave irradiation to synthesize CQDs. Moreover, the thermoelectric behavior of the composite was extensively investigated, and it was found to be tuned by CQDs concentration. Finally, we suggest that the PVA/CQD nanocomposite may have potential applications in the design of photocatalysis reactors, electromagnetic shielding, and thermoelectric power devices.

PVA (M w = 125 × 103 g/mole, polydispersity index: PDI = 0.25) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals, and d-glucose (99.8% purity) and acetone (99.9% purity) were obtained from Al-Nasr Chemical Company, Egypt.

The green PVA film (PCQ0) was fabricated by the solution casting method as follows [12]: Under experimental conditions (at 65 ± 3 °C with mechanical stirring), PVA (2 g) was liquefied in deionized water (50 ml) for 2 h, until it completely dissolved in the solution. The solution was then poured into a petri dish and air-dried for an average of 3 days, which yielded dry films with high homogeneity.

CQDs nanoparticles were prepared through the microwave heating method. In this work, d-glucose (11.1 g) was used as a carbon source and was liquefied in 100 ml of deionized water and acetone with a molar ratio of 1:1 under mechanical stirring for 0.5 h. Subsequently, the obtained solution (50 ml) was transferred to a reaction glass bottle and heated in a home-microwave reaction oven using a rotor. Microwave power of 700 W was used for the reaction, and heating time was 12 min. When the colorless solution turned into yellow color, the CQDs solution was centrifuged to obtain CQDs in a solid state.

The nanocomposite (PVA/CQD) films were synthesized through the solution casting technique [12–14]. Film PCQ1 with a CQDs content of 0.25 wt% was fabricated by dissolving CQDs in deionized water and 2 g of PVA in 50 ml of deionized water at 65 ± 3 °C for 2 h. Once PVA was completely dissolved, the CQDs solution was added at a rate of one drop per second to ensure the well dispersion of CQDs in PVA under mechanical stirring. Once the portion of water was evaporated, the solution was casted and air-dried for an average of 3 days at room temperature. Then, the obtained films were labeled as PCQ0, PCQ1, PCQ2, PCQ3, and PCQ4 corresponding to CQDs nanoparticle contents of 0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, and 2 wt%, respectively.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns for the prepared carbon quantum dot (CQDs) nanoparticles were recorded using a Bruker Axs-D8 Advance X-ray powder diffractometer with a monochromatic beam of wavelength 0.154 nm Cu Kα radiation. The sample was measured at the 2θ range between 5° and 60° at room temperature. A TEM microscope was used to determine the size of the synthesized CQDs nanoparticles. A transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Model JEOL-JEM-1230) operated at 80–100 kV was used to observe CQDs particles by placing a drop of the as-synthesized CQDs solution onto a carbon-coated copper grid. The diameter of CQDs nanoparticles was determined using Image J software. The mean diameter and the corresponding standard deviation were calculated from measurements. A Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (Model Nicolet DTGS TEC detector) was used to obtain the FTIR spectra of CQDs nanoparticles in the wavenumber range from 4000 to 400 cm− 1 to analyze the compositions of CQDs. To measure the conductivity of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films, both sides of the films were coated with silver paste on the particular area, and they were connected by two fine copper wires to measure the I–V characteristic using a high-impedance electrometer (Keithley 6517B). The DC conductivity was calculated at 10 V using the relation
σ =
d
R A
(S/cm), where d is the thickness, A is the area of the electrode, and R is the resistance of the sample. A differential scanning calorimeter (DSC-60, Shimadzu) under nitrogen (20 ml/min) was used to determine the melting point of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite. A mechanical test apparatus (AMETEK) connected with a force gauge (Hunter Spring Accu-Force II, 0.01 N resolution) was used to determine the mechanical parameters of PVA/CQDs nanocomposites. The tested films were divided into small pieces of dimensions (20 × 2 × 0.21 mm) of the films. The dimensions of the prepared films were measured using a digital caliper with an accuracy of 0.001. The thickness of the films was measured and found to be 210 μm.

The PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films were cut into strips and coated with the silver paste. One end was heated by contact with hot objects, and the other end was maintained at room temperature or without heating, as shown in Scheme R1 . Electrical potential differences were measured using a micro-voltmeter, and the temperatures were recorded using an infrared thermometer at both ends. The temperature differences between the two ends (high and low temperature ends) are approximately 10 K. The Seebeck coefficient
(
S
)
was measured in the temperature scanning range of 25–200 °C using the relation
s =
Δ V
Δ T
, where ΔV is the thermoelectromotive force (emf) produced across the test pellet sample due to a temperature difference of ΔT = 10 °C.

A homemade device was used to measure the thermal conductivity of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite as described in Scheme R1. One end of the stainless steel block was heated using an electrical resistance heater, but the other end was not heated. The PVA/CQDs nanocomposite was sandwiched between two stainless steel blocks. The overall system was encapsulated with a layer of heat insulation cotton that provides excellent thermal insulations to prevent heat from dissipating into the environment. Two stainless steel blocks can be considered as heat sinks and were connected with thermocouples at the block center with an interval of 5.00 ± 0.2 mm. PVA/CQD thermal conductivity was determined from the relation
K =
Q d
[
(
T
1
/
T
2
)
A ]
, where d is the thickness of the films, T1 is the high temperature at one end, T2 is the low temperature at the other end, A is the cross-sectional area of the films that transferred heat, and Q is the heat transfer per unit time. Only one case was obtained, where the total electric power (IV) was equal to the heat transfer Q. This case is the thermal equilibrium state that is achieved by the system, where there is no heat exchange with the surrounding environment. In addition, we need to correct the heat loss due to wire conduction and radiation, which is a more serious concern in this direct method, to achieve high accuracy, where we followed the steps given in the references [24–26]. We summarize this correction as follows: the corrected thermal conductivity was determined from
K =
Q d
A Δ T ]
and
Q = p −
Q
l o s s
, where Q is the amount of heat flowing through the sample, A is the cross-sectional area of the sample, d and
Δ T
are the distance and temperature difference between temperature sensors, P is the applied heating power at the heat source side, and Qloss is the parasitic heat losses due to radiation, conduction, and convection to ambient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray analysis and TEM and FTIR studies
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 1 (a) shows the XRD pattern of green PVA, as-prepared CQDs, and PVA/CQDs nanocomposites (sample PCQ4). It is very clear that the green PVA has a single peak at 2θ = 19.5°, which approves the semi-crystalline nature of the PVA polymer. The appearance of this peak is due to the intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonding between the PVA polymer chains [27]. On the other hand, CQDs nanoparticles showed a single broad peak at 2θ = 23.7°, which was attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms in plane C (002), which represents the hexagonal lattice of the structure of graphite (CQDs). The d-spacing of the as-prepared CQDs was determined from Bragg's equation (1), depending on the reflection peak position of the C (002).
(1)
n λ = 2 d
sin
Θ
where n is a positive integer (1), λ is the wavelength of the X-ray beam that strikes the sample (λ = 0.154 nm), and θ is the peak position of the plane C (002). The calculated d-spacing of CQDs was approximately 0.35 nm for the plane C (002). Furthermore, the broadening of the XRD peak of the CQDs was attributed to the small size of CQDs nanoparticles. This d-spacing value is approximated to the value of the d-spacing reported by Luk et al., who observed the d-spacing value to be approximately 0.33 nm [28].

However, when CQDs nanoparticles were doped to the PVA polymer to create the nanocomposite, the peak that characterized the PVA polymer (2 θ = 19.5°) increased. This indicates that the crystallinity of the PVA polymer increases with doping of the PVA with CQDs nanoparticles.

In fact, XRD analysis was conducted to study the crystallinity degree in the green PVA and PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films. Actually, the weight fraction of the crystalline portion of a polymer is called the crystallinity, and the physical (mechanical and thermal) properties of the polymers are significantly dependent on that parameter. XRD analysis is most frequently performed to determine crystallinity in polymers. There are two familiar methods to calculate the degree of crystallinity in polymers and polymer nanocomposites. The first method is based on the assumption that it is possible to separate the intensity contributions arising from the crystalline and amorphous regions. The crystallinity degree in this method is the integrated area under the crystalline regions divided by the summation of both the integrated areas of the crystalline and amorphous regions or can be defined as the ratio of the integrated area under the crystalline peaks to the integrated area under the complete XRD trace. Other measures may be used, such as the height of peaks. With proper attention to experimental detail, this method provides one of the fundamental measures of crystallinity in polymers (for more details, see Supplementary material S1). Here, we use the first method (integrated area) to determine the crystallinity of the nanocomposite films, and it was found to be 45.5 ± 1%. This increase in the crystallinity of PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films can be explained on the basis of the increase in interaction between CQDs nanoparticles and the PVA polymer chains, thus increasing the intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonding, causing the reordering of the chains in the nanocomposite, and increasing the crystallinity of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite, as shown in Fig. 1(b). A similar behavior was observed by El-Shamy et al. [12], who reported an increase in the degree of crystallinity to 48.60% for the PVA/Ag 4 wt% nanocomposite system.

TEM image analysis was performed to estimate the size of CQDs nanoparticles and their morphology, as shown in Fig. 1(c). The image shows that many small carbogenic nanoparticles that are separated and isolated from each other, and they are well distributed in the matrix of the PVA. The average diameter of CQDs was 4.5 nm, and they had a narrow size distribution ranging from 2 to 6 nm. This value is in agreement with the value of the particle size (3.42 nm) reported in Ref. [29].

To identify the composition of the as-synthesized CQDs nanoparticles, the FTIR spectra were measured, as shown in Fig. 1(D). The figure shows a band at wavenumber 1640 cm−1; this band is ascribed to the CC stretching vibration, which is the origin of CQDs core. The band at wavenumber 3391 cm−1 is ascribed to the O–H stretching. The peaks at wavenumbers 1026 and 1076 cm−1 are ascribed to the C–O in CQDs nanoparticles. The peaks at wavenumbers 1413 and 2927 cm−1 are ascribed to the C–H bonding. All peaks are summarized in Table 1 . This means that the surfaces of CQDs nanoparticles were functionalized with the –COOH and the –OH groups, a behavior similar to that reported in Ref. [30]. These functionalized (-OH) groups on the surface of CQDs facilitate the strong interaction between CQDs nanoparticles and PVA (-OH groups) and increase the hydrogen bonding, which enhances the mechanical and electrical properties of the nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical tensile strength and melting point
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 (A and B) shows Young's modulus and the tensile strength of (PVA/CQDs) nanocomposite films as a function of CQDs concentration. It is very clear that Young's modulus and the tensile strength are increased with CQDs concentration. This is due to the increase in the intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonds in the nanocomposites through the strong interaction between CQDs and PVA matrix [27], as shown in Fig. 1(B). It can also be seen that the strain at break decreases gradually with increasing concentration of CQDs nanoparticles, as shown in Fig. 2(C). Fig. 2(D) shows that the melting point of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite is increased with increasing concentration of CQDs nanoparticles. It can be proposed that this increase may be due to the increase in the intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonding that produces the reordering of the chains in the nanocomposite, thereby increasing the packing density, hence increasing the thickness of the crystallite (lamella) of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite, which needs high energy to melt, and, ultimately, increasing the melting point of the PVA PVA/CQDs nanocomposite [27,31].

The Seebeck coefficient as a function of absolute temperature plays a key role in the evaluation of actual charge transport mechanism in a solid. The Seebeck coefficient
(
S
)
of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite is plotted versus the absolute temperature in Kelvin, and the plot is depicted in Fig. 3 (a). It can be seen that there is an increase in the Seebeck coefficient of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite film with increasing concentration of CQDs nanoparticles. When different temperatures were applied to the ends of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite, the electrons (charge carriers) at the hot junction, with higher thermal velocities, were diffused to the cold junction. Hence, electromotive force (emf) was produced between the two ends. It is clear that all the examined samples showed a nearly metallic behavior. It is clear from the figure that there is a change in the Seebeck coefficient with absolute temperature T. It is clear that the positive magnitude of the Seebeck coefficient (S) was observed over the entire range of temperatures studied; thus, the majority of carriers are holes and PVA/CQDs nanocomposites are of P-type [20]. The positive value of the Seebeck coefficient is ascribed to the strong interaction and higher activation energy between CQDs (conductive filler) in the matrix of PVA. It is also clear that the linear relation is also observed between the Seebeck coefficient and the concentration of CQDs nanoparticles, which means that the Seebeck coefficient increases with increase in the concentration of CQDs nanoparticles, as depicted in Fig. 3(b). This is due to the increase in CQDs concentration, which leads to an increase in the charge carriers in the nanocomposites and increase in the thermal velocity diffusion between the two ends, thus increasing the Seebeck coefficient. Indeed, it is clear that the samples exhibited two different regions: the first region is a linear region and extended from 300 to 345 K. This is attributed to the alteration in PVA microstructure when doping with CQDs. The second region extended from 345 K to the end of the temperature range, and it showed a linear behavior, and this may be attributed to the thermal generation of the charge carrier mobility.

The thermal conductivities of PVA/CQD nanocomposites with various concentrations of CQD nanoparticles are depicted in Fig. 4 . The corrected thermal conductivity is given in Fig. 4(A), which shows the thermal conductivity of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite as a function of temperature. The PVA/CQDs nanocomposite shows an increase in thermal conductivity values with increasing temperature. This increase is ascribed to the increase in the segmental mobility of the PVA matrix during heating, thus resulting in the increase in the thermal conductivity [1].

As shown in Fig. 4(B), it is clear that the thermal conductivity of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite shows a linear enhancement (a quite increasing) with increase in the concentration of CQDs. There are significant changes in the thermal conductivity magnitudes of the nanocomposites. This implies that the phonon transport phenomenon is less influenced by the agglomeration of localized CQDs nanoparticles. The main reason underlying the higher thermal connectivity of PA/CQDs nanocomposites is the phonon scattering effect of nano-interfaces between CQDs nanoparticles and PVA [32].

All samples showed a significant increase in the thermal conductivity values with increasing concentration of CQDs nanoparticles. This is because CQDs nanoparticles produce conductive paths in the matrix of the PVA and increase the network density with increase in CQDs nanoparticles. Additionally, some oriented and crosslinking regions within the microstructure of PVA/CQDs nanocomposite will be created during the heating process and with increase in the concentration of CQDs nanoparticles. This produces a reduction in the thermal resistance and indicates free path of phonons. Moreover, the increase in the segmental mobility of the matrix of PVA occurs during heating, and hence, the thermal conductivity increases. It can be concluded that the thermal conductivity depends mainly on the concentration of CQDs nanoparticles in the PVA matrix, i.e., PVA–CQDs interface.

The thermal conductivity κ has two components:
(2)
κ = κ electron + κ phonon

As the concentration of CQDs increases, both the electronic thermal conductivity and the phonon thermal conductivity increase. It can be seen that the ratio of κ phonon/κ electron is high. Therefore, the thermal conductivity κ increases.

The power factor of PVA/CQDs nanocomposites can be determined from the equation
(3) P = σ S2
where σ is the electrical conductivity, and S is the Seebeck coefficient.

The power factor of PVA/CQDs nanocomposites was plotted as a function of the concentration of CQDs nanoparticles (Fig. 5 ). It is clear that the power factor of the nanocomposite (PVA/CQDs) increases with increasing CQDs nanoparticles. The increase in power factor is due to the increase in both the Seebeck coefficient and the conductivity of (PVA/CQDs) films.

In this PVA/CQDs nanocomposite, the decoupling of conductivity and the Seebeck coefficient can be attributed to a continuous network present in the nanocomposite, where many CQDs bridge adjacent chains of the PVA matrix with each other. This structure can provide a more effective pathway for conductive carrier transport and exerts a carrier energy-filtering effect produced by the potential barrier of energy through various interfaces between CQDs and PVA chains. It is thought that the carriers that contribute to the transport mechanism are the high-energy carriers, while those at the interfaces are low-energy carriers, which are scattered so that filtering by a potential barrier of a nano-scale can increase the power factor. In this system, carrier mobility increases more with increasing CQDs concentration. This result shows the improvement of the carrier mobility by energy filtering that permits the Seebeck coefficient to increase without destroying the conductivity, thus increasing the power factor in PVA/CQDs nanocomposites.

Fig. 6(A) shows the relationship between the electrical conductivity and the temperature. The electrical conductivity of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite increases with increase in the temperature. This may be due to the increase in temperature, especially above the glass temperature of PVA, which leads to an increase in the free charge carriers in the nanocomposites and, in turn, an increase in the electrical conductivity [13,27].

Fig. 6 (B) shows the relationship between the electrical conductivity and the concentration of CQDs nanoparticles, where the conductivity increases with increase in the concentration of CQDs nanoparticles. This can be explained on the basis of the increase in the highly active conductive CQDs nanoparticles inside the PVA chain network after CQDs nanoparticles are doped onto PVA [13]. The power factors of PVA/CQDs nanocomposites against electrical conductivity were plotted, and the plot is shown in Fig.(7) . The figure clarifies that the power factor of PVA/CQDs nanocomposites is strongly dependent on the conductivity σ, where at a higher conductivity value, a higher power factor was observed. This behavior may be attributed to the higher charge carrier mobility of CQDs that contributes to the conductivity of the nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Figure of merit of PVA/CQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 8 shows the dependence of the figure of merit of PVA/CQDs nanocomposites on the concentration of CQDs. The figure of merit ZT is determined from the following equation.
(4)
Z T =
S
2
σ T
κ
where (
S
2
σ T
) denotes the power generated by the thermoelectric nanocomposite (PVA/CQDs), and (
(
1
κ
)
) is a measure of its ability to maintain a temperature difference.

It is clear that the figure of merit of the nanocomposite (PVA/CQDs) increases (a rapid growth) with increase in the concentration of CQDs. This argument proves that CQDs embedded into the matrix of PVA improves the nanocomposite (PVA/CQDs) thermodynamic stability and network density. It is clear that the conductivity increases by an order of magnitude with increase in the concentration of CQDs, which in turn increases the Seebeck coefficient, and thus, the nanocomposites have a large figure of merit.

Table 2 shows a comparison between the (PVA/CQDs) nanocomposite system presented in the current study and other nanocomposites based on conductive and nonconductive polymer systems. It can be seen that the figure of merit value (ZT) of PVA/CQDs nanocomposites is higher than that of some nanocomposites such as PANI/SMCNT (41.4 wt%), CNT/PVAc (20 wt%), PANI-CNT (30 wt%)/PANI, and rGO/PANI-SSA (50 wt%). In addition, the ZT value of the same nanocomposite PVA/CQDs is lower than that of other nanocomposites such as PANI/SWCNT (84.2 wt%) and MWCNT/Porous PANI (1 wt%). It can be concluded from this comparison that the nanocomposite prepared in this work has some advantages when compared with other conducting polymer nanocomposites, such as thermoelectric parameters, high thermal and mechanical stability, easy processability, rapid preparation, flexibility, less raw material usage, less harmful, economical, and environmental friendly. Moreover, the ZT value of this nanocomposite does not reach to that of other nanocomposites. Finally, it can be recommended that increasing the concentration of the filler (CQDs) nanoparticles or post-treatment of the nanocomposite with acids or bases or by other methods may enhance the figure of merit of the as-synthesized nanocomposite to produce a higher conducting composite and enhance the thermoelectric ZT of the nanocomposite based on this nanomaterial.

In this work and for the first time, the nanocomposite (PVA/CQD) films were successfully synthesized by the solution casting technique. The nanocomposite (PVA/CQDs) films were characterized by XRD and TEM analyses. The parameters conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, power factor, and figure of merit were also studied in-depth as a function of CQDs nanoparticles. It was found that the Young's modulus and the tensile strength increased with increasing CQDs nanoparticles in the nanocomposite because of the increase in the intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonding. The conductivity of the PVA/CQDs nanocomposite increased with increasing CQDs nanoparticles and was found to be 30.3 S/cm at a CQDs content of 2 wt%. The Seebeck coefficient, power factor, and figure of merit were also enhanced by increasing CQDs nanoparticles in the PVA matrix. The power factor was found to be 15.2 μW/m K2 at a CQDs content of 2 wt%. The figure of merit was recorded to be 0.005 with a CQDs content of 2 wt%. Finally, the enhancement of mechanical strength, melting point, the power factor, and figure of merit of these composites makes them a candidate to be used in catalysis reactors, electromagnetic shielding, and thermoelectric power applications.
